units.title: Basics 1
units.description: Your very first German words and sentences. Learn people, food, drinks, and how to say what you are and what you eat or drink.
units.guidebook:
Welcome to German! 🎉

**Nouns & Gender:** Every German noun has a gender: **der** (masculine), **die** (feminine), or **das** (neuter). You must learn the article with the word — it's part of the word!

**The verb *sein* (to be):**
- ich bin — I am
- du bist — you are
- er/sie ist — he/she is

Example: *Ich bin ein Mann.* (I am a man.)

**Verbs *essen* (to eat) and *trinken* (to drink):**
- ich esse / trinke
- du isst / trinkst
- er/sie isst / trinkt

Example: *Ich esse Brot.* (I eat bread.)

**Word order:** German usually puts the verb in the **second position**. Time words like *Ich* come first, then the verb, then the rest.

**Tip:** Always capitalize nouns in German!

units.title: Greetings
units.description: Learn essential German greetings and polite expressions for everyday conversations.
units.guidebook:
## Greetings & Politeness 👋

German greetings change with the time of day:
- **Guten Morgen** (good morning) — until about noon
- **Guten Tag** (good day) — the safe all-day default
- **Guten Abend** (good evening) — from about 18:00
- **Gute Nacht** (good night) — only when parting or going to bed

**Hallo** is casual and works any time with friends. **Tschüss** is an informal goodbye; **Auf Wiedersehen** is more formal. To say "see you soon," use **Bis bald**.

When meeting someone, Germans often ask **Wie geht es Ihnen?** (formal) or **Wie geht's?** (casual). A typical reply is **Mir geht es gut, danke** ("I'm doing well, thanks").

**Bitte** means both "please" and "you're welcome" — context tells you which. **Danke** means "thanks," and **Vielen Dank** means "thank you very much." **Entschuldigung** ("excuse me") is used to get attention or apologize for small things; **Es tut mir leid** is a stronger apology.

Politeness goes a long way in German — use **Sie** (formal you) with strangers and **du** with friends and children.

units.title: Food
units.description: Learn essential German words for food and drink, and how to say what you want, eat, and drink.
units.guidebook:
## Talking About Food in German 🍽️

In this unit, you'll learn the most common food and drink words. German nouns have **genders** (der, die, das), and you must learn the article with each noun:

- **der** = masculine (e.g., *der Käse* – the cheese)
- **die** = feminine (e.g., *die Milch* – the milk)
- **das** = neuter (e.g., *das Ei* – the egg)

### Verbs for eating and drinking
- **essen** (to eat): *Ich esse Brot.* – I eat bread.
- **trinken** (to drink): *Ich trinke Kaffee.* – I drink coffee.
- **möchten** (to want): *Ich möchte Wasser.* – I would like water.

### Word order
German usually places the verb in the **second position** of a sentence. With *möchten*, the other verb goes to the end: *Ich möchte **essen**.*

### Plurals
Some plurals are irregular: *das Ei → die Eier* (eggs). Don't worry — you'll learn them with practice!

exercises.translation: I would like to drink coffee.

exercises.answer: He drinks a glass of water.

exercises.translation: We would like to buy fruit.

exercises.prompt: Ich esse ein Ei zum Frühstück.
exercises.audioTarget: Ich esse ein Ei zum Frühstück.

exercises.translation: I eat an egg for breakfast.
exercises.audioTarget: Ich esse ein Ei zum Frühstück.

units.title: Animals
units.description: Learn the names of common animals in German and how to describe them simply.
units.guidebook:
Welcome to **Animals**! 🐾

In German, every noun has a **gender** (der/die/das), which affects the article you use. Most animals follow these patterns:
- **der** for male animals (e.g., *der Hund*)
- **die** for female animals (e.g., *die Katze*)
- **das** for baby/young animals (e.g., *das Kalb*)

To say **"the dog is big"**, use: *Der Hund ist groß.* Notice the verb **ist** (is) comes second. To make a sentence negative, add **nicht** (not) before the adjective: *Der Hund ist nicht klein.*

To describe an animal, pair a noun with an adjective like **groß** (big), **klein** (small), **schnell** (fast), or **süß** (cute). Always capitalize nouns in German! 🐴

exercises.answer: The bird is in the tree.

exercises.prompt: Die Kuh ist auf dem Feld.
exercises.audioTarget: Die Kuh ist auf dem Feld.

exercises.translation: The fish is in the water.

exercises.prompt: Die Kuh ist auf dem Feld.
exercises.audioTarget: Die Kuh ist auf dem Feld.
exercises.answer: The cow is in the field.

units.title: Travel
units.description: Learn essential German vocabulary and phrases for getting around cities, taking trains, staying in hotels, and visiting museums.
units.guidebook:
## Getting Around in German 🇩🇪

**Asking where things are** is one of the most useful travel skills. Use:
- *Wo ist...?* — Where is...?
- *Wo ist der Bahnhof?* — Where is the train station?

**Getting directions** often starts with *Wie komme ich zu...?** (How do I get to...?). You can also use the imperative: *Gehen Sie geradeaus* (Go straight ahead) or *Biegen Sie links ab* (Turn left).

**At the hotel**, you'll need *Ich möchte ein Zimmer reservieren* (I would like to reserve a room). German nouns always carry a gender — *der Bahnhof* (masculine), *die Stadt* (feminine), *das Museum* (neuter) — so memorize articles with nouns!

**Compound nouns** are everywhere in German: *Flughafen* (airport) = *Flug* + *Hafen*. Recognizing parts helps you guess meanings.

**Politeness matters:** use *bitte* (please) and *danke* (thanks) often. *Entschuldigung* politely gets attention or means "excuse me."

Gute Reise! 🚆

exercises.answer: I would like to reserve a room.

exercises.translation: How do I get to the museum?

exercises.prompt: Der Flug geht um acht Uhr.
exercises.audioTarget: Der Flug geht um acht Uhr.

exercises.translation: The flight leaves at eight o'clock.
exercises.audioTarget: Der Flug geht um acht Uhr.
